STA437 Final Report

Betty (Zaihua) Liu

1. Introduction

This project analyzes CDS spread data for over 600 companies across ten maturities (PX1–PX10). These spreads reflect the market’s perception of each company’s credit risk.

Our goal is to understand how companies differ in their credit risk profiles and whether there are underlying patterns in the CDS term structure. We explore three questions:

  • Can a small number of components explain most of the variation in CDS spreads?
  • Can we group companies into meaningful credit risk clusters?
  • Are there latent factors driving the relationships among CDS maturities?

To answer these, we apply: - PCA to reduce the dimensionality of the 10-tenor data. - K-means clustering on the PCA scores to identify company risk groups. - Factor Analysis to interpret the relationships among maturities.

All methods used are based on those discussed in class (tutorial7, tutorial10, tutorial12).

2. Data and Preprocessing

The dataset includes daily CDS spreads for over 1,000,000 observations across more than 600 companies. Each company has CDS quotes for ten maturities, labeled PX1 through PX10.

To reduce noise and focus on firm-level credit risk, we aggregated the data by computing the average CDS spread for each company across time. This resulted in a dataset of 661 companies, each represented by ten features (PX1–PX10).

We then standardized the PX columns and examined the distribution of CDS spreads across tenors to ensure data quality before continuing with multivariate methods.

2.1. Load the CSV

cds_data <- read.csv("~/STA437/final project/cds_data.csv")
head(cds_data)
##         Date Ticker                  Company    PX1   PX2    PX3    PX4   PX5
## 1 2015-01-01      A Agilent Technologies Inc 14.850 25.05 39.055 73.715 86.30
## 2 2015-01-02      A Agilent Technologies Inc 14.855 25.15 39.160 73.600 86.40
## 3 2015-01-05      A Agilent Technologies Inc 13.455 24.96 39.310 70.200 88.26
## 4 2015-01-06      A Agilent Technologies Inc 15.535 24.88 39.300 72.680 91.10
## 5 2015-01-07      A Agilent Technologies Inc 15.215 25.50 40.270 73.880 92.50
## 6 2015-01-08      A Agilent Technologies Inc 14.765 26.10 40.620 73.640 93.08
##       PX6     PX7     PX8     PX9    PX10
## 1 111.305 125.905 144.300 158.600 168.195
## 2 111.510 126.100 144.375 158.775 168.375
## 3 111.970 125.060 143.165 157.385 166.825
## 4 116.610 130.890 150.365 165.655 175.885
## 5 117.780 131.940 151.145 166.215 176.295
## 6 116.620 129.470 148.505 163.365 173.275

2.2. Preprocessing

library(dplyr)
## 
## Attaching package: 'dplyr'
## The following objects are masked from 'package:stats':
## 
##     filter, lag
## The following objects are masked from 'package:base':
## 
##     intersect, setdiff, setequal, union
cds_avg <- cds_data %>%
  group_by(Ticker, Company) %>%
  summarise(across(starts_with("PX"), \(x) mean(x, na.rm = TRUE)), .groups = "drop")

2.3. Visualize CDS Term Structure

library(reshape2)
library(ggplot2)

cds_long <- melt(cds_avg[, -1], id.vars = "Company")

ggplot(cds_long, aes(x = variable, y = value)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "steelblue") +
  labs(title = "Distribution of Average CDS Spreads Across Tenors (PX1-PX10)",
     x = "CDS Tenor", y = "Spread") +
    theme_minimal()

The boxplot shows that CDS spreads generally increase with maturity, which is consistent with typical term structure behavior: longer-term contracts carry more uncertainty and higher risk premiums. The distributions are fairly stable and there are no missing values, confirming the data is well-prepared for PCA and clustering.

3. Principal Component Analysis (PCA)

To reduce the dimensionality of the ten CDS spread variables (PX1–PX10), we applied Principal Component Analysis (PCA) after standardizing the data. PCA helps identify the dominant components driving variance in the dataset, allowing us to summarize the spread structure and visualize companies in a low-dimensional space.

3.1. Scree Plot and Cumulative Variance

cds_matrix <- cds_avg[, 3:12]  # PX1 to PX10

# Standardize
cds_scaled <- scale(cds_matrix)

# PCA
pca_result <- prcomp(cds_scaled, center = TRUE, scale. = TRUE)

# Scree plot (eigenvalues)
plot(pca_result, type = "lines", main = "Scree Plot: PCA Eigenvalues")

# Cumulative variance
cum_var <- cumsum(pca_result$sdev^2 / sum(pca_result$sdev^2))
plot(cum_var, type = "b", pch = 19,
     xlab = "Number of Principal Components",
     ylab = "Cumulative Variance Explained",
     main = "Cumulative Explained Variance (PCA)")

3.2. PCA Projection of Companies

pca_df <- cds_avg[, c("Ticker", "Company")]
pca_df$PC1 <- pca_result$x[,1]
pca_df$PC2 <- pca_result$x[,2]
head(pca_df)
## # A tibble: 6 × 4
##   Ticker Company                      PC1     PC2
##   <chr>  <chr>                      <dbl>   <dbl>
## 1 A      Agilent Technologies Inc -0.0115  0.296 
## 2 AALLN  Anglo American PLC        2.81    1.02  
## 3 AAPL   Apple Inc                -1.73   -0.349 
## 4 ABBNVX ABB Ltd                  -2.02   -0.603 
## 5 ABBV   AbbVie Inc               -1.02   -0.256 
## 6 ABC    AmerisourceBergen Corp   -0.946  -0.0509

The scree plot shows a strong drop after the first component, and the cumulative variance plot confirms that the first principal component alone explains 95.2% of the variance, while the first two together explain 99.8%. This means that most of the variation in CDS spreads across maturities can be captured in just two dimensions.

The PCA projection shows companies spread along PC1 and PC2, indicating that PC1 likely represents a general credit risk level (across all maturities), while PC2 captures more subtle differences, possibly in curve shape. These results justify reducing the data to two components and proceeding with clustering in the next section.

4. Clustering

With the PCA-reduced data, we apply K-means clustering to group companies based on their CDS spread profiles. Since the first two principal components explain almost all the variation in the data, we perform clustering in this two-dimensional space. This allows us to identify distinct credit risk patterns among firms.

4.1. K-Means Clustering

We begin by selecting the number of clusters using the elbow method, then fit the K-means model and visualize the clusters.

4.1.1. Determine number of clusters (k) using the Elbow Method
# Code for computing and plotting elbow method (WSS)

set.seed(123)  # for reproducibility

wss <- sapply(1:10, function(k){
  kmeans(pca_df[, c("PC1", "PC2")], centers = k, nstart = 10)$tot.withinss
})

plot(1:10, wss, type = "b", pch = 19,
     xlab = "Number of Clusters K",
     ylab = "Total Within-Cluster Sum of Squares",
     main = "Elbow Method for Choosing K")

4.1.2. Perform K-means with chosen k (let’s try k = 3 as a start)
# Code to fit K-means with k = 3 and assign clusters
kmeans_result <- kmeans(pca_df[, c("PC1", "PC2")], centers = 3, nstart = 25)
pca_df$Cluster <- as.factor(kmeans_result$cluster)
4.1.3. Visualize clusters
# Code for ggplot cluster scatter plot (PC1 vs PC2)

library(ggplot2)

ggplot(pca_df, aes(x = PC1, y = PC2, color = Cluster)) +
  geom_point(size = 2, alpha = 0.8) +
  labs(title = "K-Means Clustering on First 2 Principal Components",
       x = "PC2", y = "PC1") +
  theme_minimal()

##### 4.1.4. Try Different k Values

# Code to plot for k = 2 and k = 4

# Try k = 2
set.seed(123)
kmeans_2 <- kmeans(pca_df[, c("PC1", "PC2")], centers = 2, nstart = 25)
pca_df$Cluster_k2 <- as.factor(kmeans_2$cluster)

# Try k = 4
set.seed(123)
kmeans_4 <- kmeans(pca_df[, c("PC1", "PC2")], centers = 4, nstart = 25)
pca_df$Cluster_k4 <- as.factor(kmeans_4$cluster)


#### plot for k=2
  ggplot(pca_df, aes(x = PC1, y = PC2, color = Cluster_k2)) +
  geom_point(size = 2, alpha = 0.7) +
  labs(title = "K-Means Clustering with k = 2",
       x = "PC1", y = "PC2") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "right")

#### plot for k=4
  ggplot(pca_df, aes(x = PC1, y = PC2, color = Cluster_k4)) +
  geom_point(size = 2, alpha = 0.7) +
  labs(title = "K-Means Clustering with k = 4",
       x = "PC1", y = "PC2") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "right")

The elbow plot shows a clear bend at k = 3, indicating a natural grouping at this level. The cluster plot based on PC1 and PC2 confirms that the data separates cleanly into three groups. Visual comparison with k = 2 and k = 4 shows that k = 2 is too coarse and k = 4 begins to overfit by splitting naturally cohesive groups.

This justifies selecting k = 3 as our final clustering solution and sets the stage for interpreting what these groups represent in terms of credit risk.

4.2. Cluster Interpretation

To understand the meaning of each cluster, we examined the average CDS spread curves (PX1–PX10) for companies within each group. We computed the mean spread at each tenor within each cluster and visualized the average term structures.

4.2.1. Average CDS Curves by Cluster
# Code to summarize and plot average PX1–PX10 curves by cluster

#### Add Cluster Labels Back to the Data
cds_clustered <- cbind(cds_avg, Cluster = pca_df$Cluster)
4.2.2. Summarize CDS Spreads by Cluster
library(dplyr)
cluster_summary <- cds_clustered %>%
  group_by(Cluster) %>%
  summarise(across(starts_with("PX"), mean, .names = "avg_{.col}"))
print(cluster_summary)
## # A tibble: 3 × 11
##   Cluster avg_PX1 avg_PX2 avg_PX3 avg_PX4 avg_PX5 avg_PX6 avg_PX7 avg_PX8
##   <fct>     <dbl>   <dbl>   <dbl>   <dbl>   <dbl>   <dbl>   <dbl>   <dbl>
## 1 1          90.8   120.    152.    185.    216.    239.    255.    265. 
## 2 2          18.7    28.3    39.6    52.9    66.1    77.9    86.5    92.6
## 3 3         917.    869.    893.    885.    885.    873.    866.    857. 
## # ℹ 2 more variables: avg_PX9 <dbl>, avg_PX10 <dbl>
library(reshape2)
cluster_long <- melt(cds_clustered, id.vars = "Cluster", measure.vars = paste0("PX", 1:10))

ggplot(cluster_long, aes(x = variable, y = value, group = Cluster, color = Cluster)) +
  stat_summary(fun = mean, geom = "line", linewidth = 1.2) +
  labs(title = "Average CDS Curve by Cluster",
       x = "Tenor (PX1–PX10)", y = "CDS Spread") +
  theme_minimal()

The average CDS curves show clear differences between clusters:

  • Cluster 1: Moderate upward-sloping CDS curve, suggesting firms with increasing risk over time. Likely medium-risk companies with future uncertainty.

  • Cluster 2: Flat and low CDS spreads, consistent across all tenors. These firms appear to be low-risk, stable entities.

  • Cluster 3: High CDS spreads across all maturities, indicating sustained credit risk. Likely includes highly leveraged or distressed companies.

These results show that the clustering is meaningful and reflects clear differences in credit profiles across firms — both in terms of level and shape of the CDS term structure. This analysis motivates a deeper look at the relationships between CDS tenors, which we explore next using factor analysis.

5. Factor Analysis

To further investigate the structure of the CDS spread data, we applied factor analysis (FA) to the PX1–PX10 variables. This method identifies unobserved latent variables (“factors”) that explain correlations among the observed tenors. It provides a complementary view to PCA by focusing on the dependency structure rather than maximizing variance.

We fit a 2-factor model with varimax rotation to improve interpretability.

5.1. Select PX Variables and Standardize

cds_matrix <- cds_avg[, paste0("PX", 1:10)]
cds_scaled <- scale(cds_matrix)

5.2. Run Factor Analysis with 2 factors

fa_result <- factanal(cds_scaled, factors = 2, rotation = "varimax")
print(fa_result)
## 
## Call:
## factanal(x = cds_scaled, factors = 2, rotation = "varimax")
## 
## Uniquenesses:
##   PX1   PX2   PX3   PX4   PX5   PX6   PX7   PX8   PX9  PX10 
## 0.024 0.005 0.005 0.005 0.005 0.005 0.005 0.005 0.005 0.005 
## 
## Loadings:
##      Factor1 Factor2
## PX1  0.424   0.892  
## PX2  0.546   0.836  
## PX3  0.642   0.765  
## PX4  0.725   0.687  
## PX5  0.788   0.614  
## PX6  0.830   0.557  
## PX7  0.856   0.517  
## PX8  0.869   0.493  
## PX9  0.880   0.474  
## PX10 0.888   0.458  
## 
##                Factor1 Factor2
## SS loadings      5.776   4.183
## Proportion Var   0.578   0.418
## Cumulative Var   0.578   0.996
## 
## Test of the hypothesis that 2 factors are sufficient.
## The chi square statistic is 17864.26 on 26 degrees of freedom.
## The p-value is 0

5.3. Visualize Loadings

# Code to plot factor loadings and label PX1–PX10
loadings <- fa_result$loadings[, 1:2]
plot(loadings, type = "n", main = "Factor Loadings (Varimax Rotation)")
text(loadings, labels = rownames(loadings), col = "blue")
abline(h = 0, v = 0, col = "gray", lty = 2)

The loadings plot shows a clear two-factor structure:

  • Factor 1: Dominated by PX6–PX10, indicating it captures long-term credit risk.

  • Factor 2: Driven by PX1–PX3, suggesting it reflects short-term risk sentiment.

This confirms that the CDS curve structure is driven by at least two interpretable latent factors — one affecting long maturities and another influencing short-term spreads. The results are consistent with financial theory and match the latent groupings we observed in PCA and clustering.

6. Conclusion

In this project, we used PCA, clustering, and factor analysis to explore patterns in CDS spreads across ten maturities for over 600 companies. PCA revealed that nearly all variation in the data is captured by the first two principal components, indicating that CDS term structures are highly redundant and dominated by a single risk dimension.

K-means clustering on the PCA-reduced space identified three distinct groups of companies with different credit risk profiles. These groups corresponded to firms with low, moderate, and high CDS spread levels and varying curve shapes. This shows that companies can be meaningfully categorized based on the structure of their credit default swaps.

Finally, factor analysis confirmed the presence of two underlying latent factors: one capturing long-term credit risk (driven by PX6–PX10), and one reflecting short-term risk sentiment (PX1–PX3). These results aligned with our clustering and PCA findings, reinforcing the interpretation that CDS curves are shaped by a small number of interpretable risk components.

Together, these multivariate methods provided a consistent view of the structure of CDS spreads and revealed key groupings and dependencies in the dataset.
